skill.name: notebooklm
skill.description: Query Google NotebookLM notebooks from AI coding tools using browser automation, persistent Google authentication, and local notebook library management.
skill.summary: Ask source-grounded questions against Google NotebookLM notebooks from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-command patterns; most markdown hits are command examples or API documentation, not executable backticks. The executable scripts still confirm high-risk behavior because the skill installs dependencies and a browser, automates Google NotebookLM in an authenticated Chrome profile, and stores reusable browser state containing cookies or local storage.

high_findings.title: Persistent Google Browser State Stored Locally
high_findings.description: The skill saves authenticated browser storage to disk and documents that browser cookies and session data are retained. This is necessary for the workflow, but it creates account takeover risk if the skill directory or generated data is exposed.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls storage_state with a path, and the skill documentation states that browser cookies and session data are stored. The risk is confirmed, although it appears tied to legitimate NotebookLM authentication rather than confirmed exfiltration.

high_findings.title: Automated Dependency and Browser Installation
high_findings.description: The runner creates a virtual environment, installs Python dependencies, upgrades pip, and installs a Patchright Chrome browser automatically. This increases supply-chain and execution risk for a marketplace skill because first use can fetch and execute third-party packages.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit and install requirements plus a browser runtime. The behavior is documented and purposeful, but it is high impact because package installation happens from skill execution flow.

high_findings.title: Stealth Browser Automation Against Google Services
high_findings.description: The skill depends on Patchright and configures real Chrome automation for NotebookLM login and queries. This may violate service expectations, can trigger account controls, and requires handling authenticated browser data with care.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dependency and setup comments explicitly identify Patchright and Chrome automation, while auth_manager drives an authenticated browser session. I did not find evidence of malicious network destinations, so confidence is high for risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Notebook URL Is Navigated Before Domain Validation Completes
medium_findings.description: The question script accepts a notebook URL from command-line arguments or saved library data and navigates to it before checking that the final URL is a NotebookLM domain. A malicious or mistaken URL could load unexpected content in the skill-controlled browser context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL parameter is accepted and passed to page.goto before the NotebookLM regex wait. Browser cookie scoping limits direct cookie leakage, but the ordering still creates avoidable navigation risk.

medium_findings.title: Local Cleanup Deletes Skill Data Directories
medium_findings.description: The cleanup command recursively deletes browser state and other files under the skill data directory when confirmed. The implementation scopes deletion to the skill data path and previews by default, so this is a moderate operational risk rather than malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recursive deletion is explicit and confirmed by code, but it is limited to computed cleanup paths under data_dir and has a confirmation flow unless forced.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Triggered Shell Pattern Alerts
low_findings.description: Many static findings in SKILL.md and references files are bash or Python snippets showing how to call the provided scripts. These examples are not executed by the files themselves, but they document commands that users may run manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced markdown examples or troubleshooting snippets, not runtime code. They remain relevant because the commands point to executable scripts with confirmed side effects.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Appear To Be Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto alerts appear to match ordinary words such as description, id, or session text in documentation and script metadata. I did not find evidence of cryptographic primitives being used for protection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for crypto-related APIs found no implemented weak cryptographic algorithm in the reviewed script paths. The static line hits correspond to benign text or metadata fields.

dangerous_patterns.title: Code Execution Plus Network Plus Credential Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical static heuristic is directionally valid: executable scripts run subprocess commands, perform authenticated network browser automation, and store browser session data. This combination requires human review, but I did not find confirmed malicious exfiltration or command-and-control intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All three components of the heuristic are present in executable scripts. The destinations observed are NotebookLM, Google login, package installation, and browser setup rather than a confirmed malicious endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic First-Run Environment Mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: Importing or running scripts can create a virtual environment and install dependencies or browser assets. Marketplace users should know the skill changes local filesystem state and downloads executable dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment setup and subprocess installation behavior is explicit in both executable code and user instructions. This is confirmed behavior, not merely a scanner signature.

content.value_statement: Research answers can drift when they are not grounded in your source documents. This skill connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Google NotebookLM notebooks for source-focused answers.
actual_capabilities: Checks and sets up Google NotebookLM authentication through a visible browser session.
Stores a local library of NotebookLM notebook URLs with names, topics, and descriptions.
Queries a selected NotebookLM notebook and returns the visible answer text.
Supports active notebook selection, search, listing, removal, and usage statistics.
Runs setup through a wrapper that creates a virtual environment and installs required browser automation tools.
Provides cleanup commands for stored browser state, auth metadata, and notebook library data.
limitations: Requires a Google account and manual login through a browser window.
Stores authenticated browser state locally, which must be protected by the user.
Depends on NotebookLM page structure and may break if Google changes the interface.
Uses browser automation and may be affected by account, rate, or service policy limits.

use_cases.title: Ground Product Research
use_cases.description: Ask questions against NotebookLM collections of customer notes, specs, and research documents before writing plans.

use_cases.title: Summarize Technical Sources
use_cases.description: Query uploaded documentation or architecture notes from Claude Code while keeping answers tied to selected source notebooks.

use_cases.title: Organize Knowledge Libraries
use_cases.description: Maintain named NotebookLM entries with topics and descriptions so recurring research workflows can reuse the correct notebook.

prompt_templates.title: Ask A Basic Question
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner query against the active notebook
prompt_templates.prompt: Ask my active NotebookLM notebook: What are the main conclusions in these sources?

prompt_templates.title: Add A Notebook
prompt_templates.scenario: Register a new NotebookLM URL with useful metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Add this NotebookLM notebook to my library. First discover its contents, then save it with a clear name, description, and topics.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Source Claims
prompt_templates.scenario: Use NotebookLM to check agreement across uploaded documents
prompt_templates.prompt: Query my NotebookLM notebook for where the uploaded sources agree and disagree about the launch risks.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Step Research Synthesis
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with follow-up questions and synthesis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use NotebookLM to answer this research brief. Ask follow-up questions until gaps are resolved, then synthesize the answers with source-grounded caveats.

output_examples.input: Ask NotebookLM what this policy notebook says about data retention.
output_examples.output: A concise answer summarizing the retention periods, exceptions, and unresolved points found in the notebook sources.

output_examples.input: List my saved NotebookLM notebooks and activate the security research notebook.
output_examples.output: A library view showing saved notebook names and topics, followed by confirmation that the requested notebook is active.

output_examples.input: Research the migration plan and identify missing assumptions.
output_examples.output: A synthesized research note that combines multiple NotebookLM answers and calls out assumptions that need more source evidence.

best_practices: Authenticate only in a trusted local environment and protect the generated browser state files.
Validate NotebookLM URLs before saving or querying them through the skill.
Use clear notebook descriptions and topics so the active notebook is easy to verify.
anti_patterns: Do not run this skill on shared machines without protecting Google session files.
Do not assume answers are complete without asking follow-up questions for missing details.
Do not save arbitrary URLs as notebook entries without confirming they are NotebookLM notebooks.

faq.question: Does this skill require Google login?
faq.answer: Yes. It opens a browser so the user can manually authenticate to Google NotebookLM.

faq.question: Where does it store notebook and auth data?
faq.answer: It stores library metadata and browser state under the skill data directory created on the local machine.

faq.question: Can it upload documents to NotebookLM?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow assumes the user has already added sources to NotebookLM.

faq.question: Does it call external services besides Google NotebookLM?
faq.answer: Runtime browser queries target NotebookLM, while setup can download Python packages and browser assets.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: Not without warnings and review. It handles authenticated browser state and performs automated setup commands.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
